\section{Related Work}

\textbf{Verifiers, Reward Models, and LLM-as-Judge.}
Verifiers---functions that assign rewards to agent behavior---have driven progress across diverse AI domains, from game playing to mathematical reasoning and code generation.
Recently, large language models (LLMs) and multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) have been explored as general-purpose verifiers and evaluators.
Zheng et al. [1] studied the use of strong LLMs as judges for evaluating chat assistants, identifying position, verbosity, and self-enhancement biases in LLM judges.
Liu et al. [2] proposed G-Eval, a framework using LLMs with chain-of-thought prompting for NLG evaluation, while noting a potential bias toward LLM-generated text.
Shi et al. [3] further characterized position bias in LLM judges across pairwise and listwise settings, showing it varies significantly with solution quality gaps.
Beyond general-purpose evaluation, Lambert et al. [4] introduced RewardBench, a benchmark for evaluating reward models used in RLHF, covering chat, reasoning, and safety domains.
Zhang et al. [5] proposed Generative Verifiers (GenRM), training verifiers via next-token prediction to leverage the text generation capabilities of pretrained LLMs for solution verification.
Lù et al. [6] developed AgentRewardBench, the first benchmark assessing LLM judges specifically for web agent trajectory evaluation, finding that rule-based evaluation tends to underreport agent success rates.
These works highlight known biases in LLM-based evaluation, but are primarily studied in text-only settings or with discriminative verifiers.
This motivates the target paper's investigation of MLLMs as verifiers for multimodal, open-ended agent tasks, where existing biases such as position and verbosity preferences may co-occur with a distinct over-validation tendency---agreement bias---that has not been systematically characterized.

\textbf{Agent Benchmarks and Environments.}
The development of autonomous agents has been accompanied by the creation of increasingly realistic evaluation environments.
Zhou et al. [7] introduced WebArena, a realistic web environment with fully functional websites across e-commerce, social forums, and content management domains, establishing a foundation for reproducible web agent evaluation.
Building on this, Koh et al. [8] proposed VisualWebArena (VWA), extending WebArena with visually grounded tasks requiring agents to process image-text inputs.
For desktop environments, Xie et al. [9] introduced OSWorld, a scalable real-computer benchmark supporting task setup and execution-based evaluation across operating systems and desktop applications.
Le Sellier De Chezelles et al. [10] developed the BrowserGym ecosystem, unifying web agent benchmarks under a standardized gym-like interface for consistent evaluation.
For robotic manipulation, Mandlekar et al. [11] introduced robomimic, an open-source benchmark and dataset for learning from offline human demonstrations on multi-stage manipulation tasks of varying complexity.
Collectively, these benchmarks span web, desktop, and robotic domains, providing the diverse evaluation settings used in the target paper's study of MLLM verifiers across open-ended tasks.

\textbf{Agent Architectures and Self-Improvement.}
Several agent architectures and self-improvement methods form the foundation for the target paper's experiments.
Wei et al. [12] introduced chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, demonstrating that intermediate reasoning steps significantly improve LLM performance on complex reasoning tasks.
Yao et al. [13] proposed ReAct, which interleaves reasoning traces with task-specific actions, enabling LLMs to induce and update action plans while interacting with external environments.
Shinn et al. [14] introduced Reflexion, a framework where language agents improve through verbal reflection on task feedback, maintaining reflective text in episodic memory for better decision-making in subsequent trials.
For robot learning, Chi et al. [15] proposed Diffusion Policy, representing visuomotor policies as conditional denoising diffusion processes, achieving strong performance across manipulation benchmarks including robomimic.
Yao et al. and Shinn et al. anchor the self-improvement and agent reasoning pipelines evaluated in the target paper, where MLLM verifiers provide the feedback signals that drive agent refinement.
However, the effectiveness of these pipelines depends critically on verifier quality: biased or overly permissive feedback can limit agents' ability to correct flawed behavior.
The target paper examines this dependency and proposes Self-Grounded Verification to improve verifier alignment for downstream self-improvement and online supervision.

s2_metadata.title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper systematically evaluates LLM-as-a-judge using GPT-4 to approximate human preferences in open-ended, multi-turn dialogues, revealing over 80% agreement with human ratings across controlled expert and crowdsourced evaluations. It identifies and mitigates biases such as position, morbidity, and self-enhancement effects, demonstrating that LLM judges can reliably assess alignment with human preferences—enabling scalable, automated evaluation of conversational quality beyond traditional capability benchmarks.

references.raw_text: [2] Yang Liu, Dan Iter, Yichong Xu, Shuohang Wang, Ruochen Xu, and Chenguang Zhu. "G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using GPT-4 with Better Human Alignment." arXiv:2303.16634, 2023.
references.title: G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using GPT-4 with Better Human Alignment

s2_metadata.title: G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using GPT-4 with Better Human Alignment

tldr.text: [research paper] G-Eval introduces a chain-of-thought (CoT)-enhanced, form-filling framework using GPT-4 to evaluate NLG outputs, achieving a Spearman correlation of 0.514 with human judgments on summarization and dialogue tasks—significantly outperforming prior LLM-based and reference-based metrics. By generating detailed evaluation steps via auto-CoT and leveraging token-level probability weighting, it produces fine-grained, continuous scores that reduce score skew and ties, while revealing a critical bias: LLM evaluators favor LLM-generated text over human-written content, raising concerns about self-reinforcement in training loops.

references.raw_text: [3] Lin Shi, Chiyu Ma, Wenhua Liang, Xingjian Diao, Weicheng Ma, and Soroush Vosoughi. "Judging the Judges: A Systematic Study of Position Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge." arXiv:2406.07791, 2024.
references.title: Judging the Judges: A Systematic Study of Position Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge

s2_metadata.title: Judging the Judges: A Systematic Study of Position Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge

tldr.text: [systematic study / empirical research] This paper conducts a large-scale empirical investigation into position bias in LLM-as-a-Judge systems, identifying that bias is not random and is strongly influenced by solution quality gaps rather than prompt length. It introduces three novel metrics—position consistency, preference fairness, and repetition stability—to quantify bias across pairwise and list-wise comparison settings, revealing that bias varies significantly by judge, task, and candidate quality. The study identifies Judge-Level, Candidate-Level, and Task-Level factors driving bias, and finds that disagreement among LLM judges correlates with judging difficulty, suggesting that dataset design should prioritize balanced difficulty to mitigate bias.

references.raw_text: [4] Nathan Lambert, Valentina Pyatkin, Jacob Morrison, LJ Miranda, Bill Yuchen Lin, Khyathi Chandu, Nouha Dziri, Sachin Kumar, Tom Zick, Yejin Choi, Noah A. Smith, and Hannaneh Hajishirzi. "RewardBench: Evaluating Reward Models for Language Modeling." arXiv:2403.13787, 2024.
references.title: RewardBench: Evaluating Reward Models for Language Modeling

s2_metadata.title: RewardBench: Evaluating Reward Models for Language Modeling

tldr.text: [research paper] REWARDBENCH introduces a comprehensive benchmark dataset and evaluation framework for reward models (RMs) in RLHF, featuring prompt-chosen-rejected trios across chat, reasoning, and safety domains with structured, verifiable differences (e.g., factual errors, bugs). It evaluates over 80 RMs—including classifier-based and DPO-trained models—revealing systematic shortcomings in reasoning, refusal behavior, and generalization to out-of-distribution queries, while exposing limitations in existing preference datasets that lack fine-grained, subtle preference signals.

references.raw_text: [5] Lunjun Zhang, Arian Hosseini, Hritik Bansal, Mehran Kazemi, Aviral Kumar, and Rishabh Agarwal. "Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction." arXiv:2408.15240, 2024.
references.title: Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction

s2_metadata.title: Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper proposes Generative Reward Models (GenRM) trained via next-token prediction instead of discriminative scoring, enabling LLMs to perform reasoning-based verification through chain-of-thought (CoT) rationales. GenRM-CoT explicitly generates verification rationales before predicting correctness, allowing for majority voting at test time to improve accuracy—outperforming discriminative RMs, LLM-as-a-Judge, and self-consistency by 20–30 percentage points on GSM8K and MATH/MMLU, with significant gains in error detection for subtle logical flaws (e.g., misreading 'each' in pricing problems). The method leverages inherent generative capabilities of LLMs for unified instruction tuning, CoT reasoning, and scalable verification via test-time inference.

"AgentRewardBench: Evaluating Automatic Evaluations of Web Agent Trajectories." arXiv:2504.08942, 2025.

authors: Dzmitry Bahdanau, ; Jackie Chi Kit Cheung

s2_metadata.title: AgentRewardBench: Evaluating Automatic Evaluations of Web Agent Trajectories

tldr.text: [research paper] AgentRewardBench introduces the first comprehensive benchmark to evaluate LLM judges' effectiveness in assessing web agent trajectories, comprising 1302 expert-annotated trajectories across 5 diverse environments and 4 LLMs. It reveals that no LLM consistently outperforms others across benchmarks and demonstrates that rule-based evaluation systematically underestimates agent success, exposing a critical gap in current automatic evaluation methods. The benchmark enables rigorous, human-aligned assessment of trajectory success, side effects, and repetitiveness, providing a foundation for improving LLM-based reward modeling in web agents.

references.raw_text: [7] Shuyan Zhou, Frank F. Xu, Hao Zhu, Xuhui Zhou, Robert Lo, Arun Sridhar, Xianyi Cheng, Tianyue Ou, Yonatan Bisk, Daniel Fried, Uri Alon, and Graham Neubig. "WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents." arXiv:2307.13854, 2023.
references.title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents

s2_metadata.title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents

tldr.text: [research paper] WebArena introduces a realistic, self-hosted web environment with four functional domains (e-commerce, social forums, development, content management) and embedded tools/knowledge bases to simulate human-like task execution. It features 812 long-horizon, high-level natural language tasks evaluated by functional correctness—measuring whether task outcomes achieve intended goals—rather than surface-level action matching. GPT-4 achieves only 14.41% end-to-end success, far below human performance (78.24%), revealing critical gaps in current agents' capabilities for active exploration, failure recovery, and long-term planning in complex real-world scenarios.

references.raw_text: [8] Jing Yu Koh, Robert Lo, Lawrence Jang, Vikram Duvvur, Ming Chong Lim, Po-Yu Huang, Graham Neubig, Shuyan Zhou, Ruslan Salakhutdinov, and Daniel Fried. "VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks." arXiv:2401.13649, 2024.
references.title: VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks

s2_metadata.title: VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks

tldr.text: [research paper] VisualWebArena introduces a benchmark of 910 visually grounded web tasks across Classifieds, Shopping, and Reddit environments, revealing that state-of-the-art vision-language models (VLMs) outperform text-only LLMs in real-world web navigation, with the best VLM achieving only 16.4% success (vs. 88.7% human performance). A novel VLM agent based on Set-of-Marks prompting simplifies action space representation via JavaScript-generated marks, significantly improving performance on visually complex sites by enabling direct visual grounding and action execution.

tldr.text: [research paper] OSWORLD introduces the first scalable, real-world executable environment for multimodal agents, enabling open-ended, cross-platform computer task evaluation across Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS. It features 369 real-world tasks involving multi-app workflows, GUI/CLI interactions, and file operations, with execution-based evaluation scripts and human-annotated initial states. State-of-the-art LLM/VLM agents achieve only 12.24% success—far below human performance (72.36%)—highlighting critical deficiencies in GUI grounding, operational knowledge, and action prediction, especially in complex, dynamic workflows.

tldr.text: [preprint] The paper presents a novel framework for long-form video understanding through a hierarchical agentic memory architecture that enables temporal reasoning and context retention across extended sequences. It introduces a dynamic memory module that selectively retains and reuses salient events, combined with a temporal attention mechanism that prioritizes causally relevant segments, achieving superior performance in action prediction and event coherence tasks compared to baseline models without explicit memory management.

s2_metadata.title: What Matters in Learning from Offline Human Demonstrations for Robot Manipulation

tldr.text: [research paper] A systematic evaluation of six offline learning algorithms on human-demonstrated robot manipulation tasks reveals that history-dependent models (e.g., BC-RNN, HBC) outperform state-of-the-art batch RL methods like CQL and IRIS, especially on complex, multi-stage tasks with non-Markovian human behavior. Performance is highly sensitive to dataset quality, observation space design, and stopping criteria, with human-provided data enabling successful learning on real-world tasks where machine-generated data fails. The study highlights the critical role of temporal context and data diversity in bridging the gap between human and robotic manipulation capabilities.

s2_metadata.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models

tldr.text: [research paper] Chain-of-thought prompting enables large language models to perform complex reasoning by leveraging few-shot exemplars that include intermediate natural language reasoning steps. Empirical results show that even a small number of chain-of-thought demonstrations—such as eight—can significantly boost performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks, with PaLM 540B achieving state-of-the-art results on GSM8K without fine-tuning, demonstrating that reasoning emerges naturally in sufficiently large models through in-context learning.

references.raw_text: [13] Shunyu Yao, Jeffrey Zhao, Dian Yu, Nan Du, Izhak Shafran, Karthik Narasimhan, and Yuan Cao. "ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models." arXiv:2210.03629, 2022.
references.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

s2_metadata.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

tldr.text: [research paper] ReAct introduces a prompt-based paradigm where large language models generate interleaved reasoning traces and actions, enabling dynamic, grounded reasoning through interaction with external environments. By allowing reasoning to inform action and action to refine reasoning (reason-to-act and act-to-reason), ReAct mitigates hallucination and error propagation in question answering and fact verification, while achieving significant performance gains—up to 34% and 10% absolute success rate improvements—over imitation and reinforcement learning on interactive tasks like ALFWorld and WebShop, even with one or two in-context examples. The approach enhances model interpretability by making internal reasoning and external interactions explicitly traceable to humans.

s2_metadata.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

tldr.text: [research paper] Reflexion introduces a verbal reinforcement framework where language agents generate reflective textual summaries from environmental feedback (binary or scalar) and store them in episodic memory to guide future decisions, eliminating the need for model fine-tuning. The agent leverages self-evaluation—via heuristics, binary classification, or self-written unit tests—to convert feedback into actionable linguistic insights, enabling significant performance gains in decision-making (AlfWorld +22%), reasoning (HotPotQA +20%), and programming (HumanEval +11%) without traditional reinforcement learning. The method supports diverse feedback types and sources, and is validated across tasks with ablation studies, demonstrating that reflective memory and semantic gradient signals drive iterative improvement through human-like self-correction.

s2_metadata.title: Diffusion Policy: Visuomotor Policy Learning via Action Diffusion

tldr.text: [research paper] Diffusion Policy introduces a conditional denoising diffusion framework for visuomotor policy learning in robotics, where robot actions are generated by iteratively refining noise via a learned gradient field of an action score function. It achieves superior performance—46.9% average improvement over state-of-the-art methods—by enabling stable training, modeling multimodal action distributions, and supporting high-dimensional, sequential action outputs through receding-horizon control, visual conditioning, and a time-series diffusion transformer that mitigates over-smoothing in high-frequency motion tasks.

\section{Related Work} \label{sec:related}
\looseness=-1
\textbf{MLLMs as Evaluators.}
(M)LLMs have been employed as evaluators of model outputs in various scenarios under various names---(M)LLMs as judges, critics, reward models, value functions.
This work focuses on multimodal and environment-interaction scenarios.
MLLMs have been used to score and filter agent trajectories for subsequent use in finetuning~, and test-time refinements such as to induce prompts, reflections, and tools~.
They have also served as a source of real-time feedback by producing natural language ``critiques''~, scores to rank action proposals in search~, and rewards for training~.

\looseness=-1
\textbf{AI Agents.}
There is growing interest in building AI agents to act in various environments, including the web~, mobile phones~, and computers~.
In this field, the work most closely related to ours is~, which uses a GPT-4V-based evaluator, prompted with benchmark-specific rubrics, to evaluate trajectories for Reflexion~ and behavior cloning.
Following works employ a similar evaluator to guide tree search~, filter trajectories to generate text-based memories or tools~ to boost agent performance in (Visual)WebArena~, and for RL training in simpler environments~.

\looseness=-1
\textbf{Test-time scaling} (TTS) is a major paradigm to improve model performance without increasing parameters~.
Early work~ shows that prompting LLMs to generate ``chains-of-thought'' yields substantial gains in reasoning-oriented tasks.
This idea has been extended to several settings, including multimodal~ and environment-interaction~.
Orthogonal approaches scale test-time compute via sampling and search~, where multiple generations are selected through heuristics~ or verifiers~.
Recent work leverages sampling, RL, and formal verifiers to train (M)LLMs that autonomously generate reasoning traces~.
Extending these methods to open-ended problems requires flexible and multimodal verification, for which MLLMs offer an appealing solution.

Compared to this body of work, we
\textbf{(1)} consolidate disparate applications of MLLM evaluators under a shared framework based on multimodal rewards derived from MLLM verifiers.
\textbf{(2)} Evaluate MLLM verifiers across a broader range of models, multimodal benchmarks, agents, TTS techniques, evaluation templates, and applications.
\textbf{(3)} Dissect MLLM verifier performance over fine-grained metrics, offering guidance on how to measure the quality of their evaluations and artifacts derived from them.
\textbf{(4)} Identify agreement bias, show its resilience to TTS techniques, and the risks it poses for downstream applications.
\textbf{(5)} Discuss several design choices for building MLLM verifiers and introduce SGV, a lightweight method easy to integrate into pipelines involving MLLM verifiers.
\textbf{(6)} Improve MLLM verification with benefits extending to downstream applications such as online supervision and self-improvement, achieving a new state of the art on VisualWebArena.

references.title: InSTA: Towards Internet-Scale Training For Agents

s2_metadata.title: InSTA: Towards Internet-Scale Training For Agents

tldr.text: [research paper] InSTA introduces an internet-scale training pipeline for web agents that replaces human annotations with LLM-driven task generation, safety filtering, and trajectory evaluation. It annotates 150k real-world sites with agentic tasks, generates 2.2M action traces and 150k judged trajectories, and trains small Qwen 3 1.7B models to achieve 56.9% success rate—outperforming a 235B Qwen 3 policy and reaching 94.7% of Gemini 2.5 Flash’s performance—without relying on human-curated data or closed-loop test environments.

references.label: pan2024autonomous
references.title: Autonomous Evaluation and Refinement of Digital Agents

s2_metadata.title: Autonomous Evaluation and Refinement of Digital Agents

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper presents domain-general neural evaluators that automatically assess digital agent trajectories in web navigation and device control tasks, achieving up to 92.9% agreement with oracle metrics. Two evaluation models—caption-then-reason and end-to-end VLM-based—are compared for accuracy, cost, and modularity; these evaluators are then used to refine agents via inference-time guidance or behavior cloning, resulting in 29% relative performance gains on WebArena and ~75% improvements in device control settings without any additional supervision or demonstrations.

references.title: Vision Language Models are In-Context Value Learners

s2_metadata.title: Vision Language Models are In-Context Value Learners
s2_metadata.abstract: Predicting temporal progress from visual trajectories is important for intelligent robots that can learn, adapt, and improve. However, learning such progress estimator, or temporal value function, across different tasks and domains requires both a large amount of diverse data and methods which can scale and generalize. To address these challenges, we present Generative Value Learning (\\GVL), a universal value function estimator that leverages the world knowledge embedded in vision-language models (VLMs) to predict task progress. Naively asking a VLM to predict values for a video sequence performs poorly due to the strong temporal correlation between successive frames. Instead, GVL poses value estimation as a temporal ordering problem over shuffled video frames; this seemingly more challenging task encourages VLMs to more fully exploit their underlying semantic and temporal grounding capabilities to differentiate frames based on their perceived task progress, consequently producing significantly better value predictions. Without any robot or task specific training, GVL can in-context zero-shot and few-shot predict effective values for more than 300 distinct real-world tasks across diverse robot platforms, including challenging bimanual manipulation tasks. Furthermore, we demonstrate that GVL permits flexible multi-modal in-context learning via examples from heterogeneous tasks and embodiments, such as human videos. The generality of GVL enables various downstream applications pertinent to visuomotor policy learning, including dataset filtering, success detection, and advantage-weighted regression -- all without any model training or finetuning.

tldr.text: [research paper] Generative Value Learning (GVL) introduces a novel in-context value estimation framework that leverages vision-language models (VLMs) by posing temporal progress prediction as a shuffled-frame ordering problem. By auto-regressively predicting task completion percentages over randomly shuffled video frames, GVL overcomes the inherent temporal bias in sequential video data, enabling zero-shot and few-shot generalization across 300+ real-world robotic tasks without task-specific training. The method achieves strong performance via multi-modal in-context learning (e.g., human videos) and enables downstream applications such as dataset filtering, success detection, and advantage-weighted regression through a new metric—Value-Order Correlation (VOC)—that measures alignment with ground-truth temporal order.

references.label: wang2024agentworkflowmemory
references.title: Agent Workflow Memory

s2_metadata.title: Agent Workflow Memory
s2_metadata.abstract: Despite the potential of language model-based agents to solve real-world tasks such as web navigation, current methods still struggle with long-horizon tasks with complex action trajectories. In contrast, humans can flexibly solve complex tasks by learning reusable task workflows from past experiences and using them to guide future actions. To build agents that can similarly benefit from this process, we introduce Agent Workflow Memory (AWM), a method for inducing commonly reused routines, i.e., workflows, and selectively providing workflows to the agent to guide subsequent generations. AWM flexibly applies to both offline and online scenarios, where agents induce workflows from training examples beforehand or from test queries on the fly. We experiment on two major web navigation benchmarks -- Mind2Web and WebArena -- that collectively cover 1000+ tasks from 200+ domains across travel, shopping, and social media, among others. AWM substantially improves the baseline results by 24.6% and 51.1% relative success rate on Mind2Web and WebArena while reducing the number of steps taken to solve WebArena tasks successfully. Furthermore, online AWM robustly generalizes in cross-task, website, and domain evaluations, surpassing baselines from 8.9 to 14.0 absolute points as train-test task distribution gaps widen.

tldr.text: [research paper] Agent Workflow Memory (AWM) introduces a memory mechanism that extracts reusable task routines—called workflows—from agent trajectories to guide future task execution. It induces workflows from either offline training examples or online self-generated actions, enabling continual learning of reusable subroutines (e.g., 'find a place by its name') that compose more complex routines (e.g., 'get zip code of a place'), leading to a snowball effect in performance. AWM achieves up to 51.1% relative success improvement on WebArena and 24.6% on Mind2Web, with strong generalization across tasks, websites, and domains, even under large distribution shifts.

references.label: yu2025exact
references.title: ExAct: A Video-Language Benchmark for Expert Action Analysis

s2_metadata.title: ExAct: A Video-Language Benchmark for Expert Action Analysis
s2_metadata.abstract: We present ExAct, a new video-language benchmark for expert-level understanding of skilled physical human activities. Our new benchmark contains 3521 expert-curated video question-answer pairs spanning 11 physical activities in 6 domains: Sports, Bike Repair, Cooking, Health, Music, and Dance. ExAct requires the correct answer to be selected from five carefully designed candidate options, thus necessitating a nuanced, fine-grained, expert-level understanding of physical human skills. Evaluating the recent state-of-the-art VLMs on ExAct reveals a substantial performance gap relative to human expert performance. Specifically, the best-performing GPT-4o model achieves only 44.70% accuracy, well below the 82.02% attained by trained human specialists/experts. We believe that ExAct will be beneficial for developing and evaluating VLMs capable of precise understanding of human skills in various physical and procedural domains. Dataset and code are available at https://texaser.github.io/exact_project_page/

tldr.text: [research paper] Introduces a novel framework for long-form video understanding that leverages temporal memory graphs to capture and reason over extended video sequences, enabling accurate event detection and action recognition across diverse temporal scales. The method combines a dynamic memory module with a graph-based temporal reasoning engine to model both local motion patterns and global event structures, outperforming baseline models on multi-scene and long-duration video benchmarks by leveraging structured temporal dependencies and memory replay mechanisms.

references.label: shinn2023reflexion
references.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

s2_metadata.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
s2_metadata.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have been increasingly used to interact with external environments (e.g., games, compilers, APIs) as goal-driven agents. However, it remains challenging for these language agents to quickly and efficiently learn from trial-and-error as traditional reinforcement learning methods require extensive training samples and expensive model fine-tuning. We propose Reflexion, a novel framework to reinforce language agents not by updating weights, but instead through linguistic feedback. Concretely, Reflexion agents verbally reflect on task feedback signals, then maintain their own reflective text in an episodic memory buffer to induce better decision-making in subsequent trials. Reflexion is flexible enough to incorporate various types (scalar values or free-form language) and sources (external or internally simulated) of feedback signals, and obtains significant improvements over a baseline agent across diverse tasks (sequential decision-making, coding, language reasoning). For example, Reflexion achieves a 91% pass@1 accuracy on the HumanEval coding benchmark, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art GPT-4 that achieves 80%. We also conduct ablation and analysis studies using different feedback signals, feedback incorporation methods, and agent types, and provide insights into how they affect performance.

tldr.text: [research paper] Reflexion introduces a verbal reinforcement framework where language agents generate reflective textual summaries from environmental feedback (binary or scalar) and store them in episodic memory to guide future decisions, eliminating the need for model fine-tuning. The agent leverages self-evaluation—via heuristics, binary classification, or self-written unit tests—to convert feedback into actionable linguistic insights, enabling significant performance gains in decision-making (AlfWorld +22%), reasoning (HotPotQA +20%), and programming (HumanEval +11%) without traditional reinforcement learning. The method supports diverse feedback types and sources, and is validated across tasks with ablation studies, demonstrating that reflective memory and semantic gradient signals drive iterative improvement through human-like self-correction.

references.title: VLM Agents Generate Their Own Memories: Distilling Experience into Embodied Programs of Thought

s2_metadata.title: VLM Agents Generate Their Own Memories: Distilling Experience into Embodied Programs of Thought
s2_metadata.abstract: Large-scale generative language and vision-language models (LLMs and VLMs) excel in few-shot learning but require high-quality demonstrations. We propose In-Context Abstraction Learning (ICAL), enabling VLM agents to transform suboptimal trajectories into high-quality training data through self-reflection and human feedback. Given imperfect task demonstrations, a VLM abstracts trajectories into generalized strategies and action annotations by correcting inefficiencies and annotating cognitive abstractions: causal relationships, object state changes, temporal subgoals, and task-relevant visual elements. These annotations are iteratively refined through human feedback during execution in similar environments. The resulting examples significantly improve decision-making when used for retrieval-augmented generation or fine-tuning. As the agent's example library grows, it becomes more efficient at abstracting new examples, requiring less human feedback and fewer environment interactions. ICAL achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks. In TEACh dialogue-based instruction following, combining fine-tuning and retrieval on ICAL examples outperforms raw human demonstrations and expert examples by 17.5% in goal-condition success. In VisualWebArena, retrieval-augmented GPT-4V with ICAL improves task success 1.6x, while fine-tuned Qwen2-VL achieves 2.8x improvement over the base model. In Ego4D action forecasting, we surpass few-shot GPT-4V and remain competitive with supervised models. Our approach scales 2x better than raw demonstrations and significantly reduces manual prompt engineering requirements.

tldr.text: [research paper] ICAL enables VLM agents to autonomously distill suboptimal visual trajectories into multimodal 'programs of thought' by extracting four types of cognitive abstractions—causal relationships, object state changes, temporal subgoals, and task-relevant visual elements—through self-reflection and iterative human feedback. These abstractions are stored in an evolving example library, enabling the agent to refine its reasoning, reduce reliance on raw demonstrations, and achieve 1.6x–2.8x performance gains across TEACh, VisualWebArena, and Ego4D benchmarks, with scalability improvements of up to 2x over raw demonstrations and 71.6% less human feedback per example over time.

references.label: pan2024webcanvas
references.title: WebCanvas: Benchmarking Web Agents in Online Environments

s2_metadata.title: WebCanvas: Benchmarking Web Agents in Online Environments

tldr.text: [research paper] WebCanvas introduces a dynamic online evaluation framework for web agents that addresses the gap between static benchmarks and real-world web evolution. It defines 'key nodes'—essential, path-independent milestones in task completion—to enable progress-aware, intermediate-level evaluation, and introduces Mind2Web-Live, a dataset of 542 tasks with 2,439 annotated key node states. The framework supports community-driven annotation, real-time data validation via automated monitoring, and reveals that models performing well in static environments (e.g., Mind2Web) fail in live settings due to web changes, with GPT-4-turbo + ReAct achieving 23.1% task success in live evaluations. Key node annotations serve as effective intermediate rewards, but even advanced agents struggle to generate them accurately without human guidance.

references.title: Tree Search for Language Model Agents

s2_metadata.title: Tree Search for Language Model Agents
s2_metadata.abstract: Autonomous agents powered by language models (LMs) have demonstrated promise in their ability to perform decision-making tasks such as web automation. However, a key limitation remains: LMs, primarily optimized for natural language understanding and generation, struggle with multi-step reasoning, planning, and using environmental feedback when attempting to solve realistic computer tasks. Towards addressing this, we propose an inference-time search algorithm for LM agents to explicitly perform exploration and multi-step planning in interactive web environments. Our approach is a form of best-first tree search that operates within the actual environment space, and is complementary with most existing state-of-the-art agents. It is the first tree search algorithm for LM agents that shows effectiveness on realistic web tasks. On the challenging VisualWebArena benchmark, applying our search algorithm on top of a GPT-4o agent yields a 39.7% relative increase in success rate compared to the same baseline without search, setting a state-of-the-art success rate of 26.4%. On WebArena, search also yields a 28.0% relative improvement over a baseline agent, setting a competitive success rate of 19.2%. Our experiments highlight the effectiveness of search for web agents, and we demonstrate that performance scales with increased test-time compute. We conduct a thorough analysis of our results to highlight improvements from search, limitations, and promising directions for future work. Our code and models are publicly released at https://jykoh.com/search-agents.

tldr.text: [research paper] Introduces a best-first tree search algorithm for language model agents operating in realistic web environments, enabling multi-step planning and exploration via environmental feedback. The method constructs an environment-space search graph using a model-based value function that scores trajectories by marginalizing over reasoning chains from a multimodal LM, leading to a 39.7% relative success rate improvement on VisualWebArena (SOTA) and 28.0% on WebArena, with performance scaling with test-time compute.

references.title: AgentOccam: A Simple Yet Strong Baseline for LLM-Based Web Agents

s2_metadata.title: AgentOccam: A Simple Yet Strong Baseline for LLM-Based Web Agents
s2_metadata.abstract: Autonomy via agents using large language models (LLMs) for personalized, standardized tasks boosts human efficiency. Automating web tasks (like booking hotels within a budget) is increasingly sought after. Fulfilling practical needs, the web agent also serves as an important proof-of-concept example for various agent grounding scenarios, with its success promising advancements in many future applications. Prior research often handcrafts web agent strategies (e.g., prompting templates, multi-agent systems, search methods, etc.) and the corresponding in-context examples, which may not generalize well across all real-world scenarios. On the other hand, there has been limited study on the misalignment between a web agent's observation/action representation and the pre-training data of the LLM it's based on. This discrepancy is especially notable when LLMs are primarily trained for language completion rather than tasks involving embodied navigation actions and symbolic web elements. Our study enhances an LLM-based web agent by simply refining its observation and action space to better align with the LLM's capabilities. This approach enables our base agent to significantly outperform previous methods on a wide variety of web tasks. Specifically, on WebArena, a benchmark featuring general-purpose web interaction tasks, our agent AgentOccam surpasses the previous state-of-the-art and concurrent work by 9.8 (+29.4%) and 5.9 (+15.8%) absolute points respectively, and boosts the success rate by 26.6 points (+161%) over similar plain web agents with its observation and action space alignment. We achieve this without using in-context examples, new agent roles, online feedback or search strategies. AgentOccam's simple design highlights LLMs' impressive zero-shot performance on web tasks, and underlines the critical role of carefully tuning observation and action spaces for LLM-based agents.

tldr.text: [research paper] AgentOccam achieves state-of-the-art performance on web task benchmarks by simplifying the observation and action spaces of LLM-based agents without relying on task-specific prompting, in-context examples, or online search. The method reduces redundant web elements in observations, eliminates non-essential navigation actions, and introduces planning actions (branch/prune) to enable self-organized navigation via a planning tree, which aligns the agent’s interaction space with the LLM’s pre-trained language reasoning capabilities. This design enables strong zero-shot generalization across diverse websites and real-world web tasks, outperforming prior methods by up to +29.4% on WebArena and +4.6% on WebVoyager’s definite-answer subset, demonstrating that effective agent performance stems from better representation alignment rather than complex policy engineering.

references.title: MM-Verify: Enhancing Multimodal Reasoning with Chain-of-Thought Verification

s2_metadata.title: MM-Verify: Enhancing Multimodal Reasoning with Chain-of-Thought Verification
s2_metadata.venue: Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
s2_metadata.abstract: According to the Test-Time Scaling, the integration of External Slow-Thinking with the Verify mechanism has been demonstrated to enhance multi-round reasoning in large language models (LLMs). However, in the multimodal (MM) domain, there is still a lack of a strong MM-Verifier. In this paper, we introduce MM-Verifier and MM-Reasoner to enhance multimodal reasoning through longer inference and more robust verification. First, we propose a two-step MM verification data synthesis method, which combines a simulation-based tree search with verification and uses rejection sampling to generate high-quality Chain-of-Thought (COT) data. This data is then used to fine-tune the verification model, MM-Verifier. Additionally, we present a more efficient method for synthesizing MMCOT data, bridging the gap between text-based and multimodal reasoning. The synthesized data is used to fine-tune MM-Reasoner. Our MM-Verifier outperforms all larger models on the MathCheck, MathVista, and MathVerse benchmarks. Moreover, MM-Reasoner demonstrates strong effectiveness and scalability, with performance improving as data size increases. Finally, our approach achieves strong performance when combining MM-Reasoner and MM-Verifier, reaching an accuracy of 65.3 on MathVista, surpassing GPT-4o (63.8) with 12 rollouts.

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper proposes MM-Verifier and MM-Reasoner to enhance multimodal reasoning through synthetic long-chain-of-thought (COT) data generation via simulation-based tree search and rejection sampling, enabling robust verification and scalable reasoning. A two-step data synthesis pipeline combines text-based pattern simulation with multimodal geometric reasoning, producing high-quality MMCOT data that is used to train both a multimodal verifier (MM-Verifier) and a reasoning model (MM-Reasoner), with MM-Verifier achieving SOTA performance on MathCheck, MathVista, and MathVerse benchmarks, and the combined system outperforming GPT-4o on MathVista with 12 rollouts.

references.label: koh2024visualwebarena
references.title: VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks

s2_metadata.title: VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks

tldr.text: [research paper] VisualWebArena introduces a benchmark of 910 visually grounded web tasks across Classifieds, Shopping, and Reddit environments, revealing that state-of-the-art vision-language models (VLMs) outperform text-only LLMs in real-world web navigation, with the best VLM achieving only 16.4% success (vs. 88.7% human performance). A novel VLM agent based on Set-of-Marks prompting simplifies action space representation via JavaScript-generated marks, significantly improving performance on visually complex sites by enabling direct visual grounding and action execution.

references.label: li2024effects
references.title: On the Effects of Data Scale on UI Control Agents

s2_metadata.title: On the Effects of Data Scale on UI Control Agents

tldr.text: [empirical study with dataset construction and scalability analysis] AndroidControl introduces a large-scale, diverse dataset of 15,283 human demonstrations of Android app tasks, each annotated with both high-level and low-level instructions. The study demonstrates that fine-tuned UI control agents scale effectively in-domain, with performance improving linearly with data volume, but exhibit significantly slower out-of-domain scaling—especially for high-level tasks—suggesting that data alone is insufficient for robust generalization beyond training distribution; this implies the need for additional techniques like task decomposition or domain adaptation for real-world deployment.

references.label: liu2023agentbench
references.title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents

s2_metadata.title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents

tldr.text: [research paper] AGENTBENCH introduces a multi-dimensional, real-world benchmark evaluating LLMs as agents across eight diverse environments—code, game, and web-based tasks—revealing significant performance gaps between top commercial LLMs and open-source models. The benchmark identifies poor long-term reasoning, decision-making, and instruction following as primary failure modes, with performance improvements observed when LLMs are trained on code and high-quality multi-turn alignment data. Results highlight the need for structured evaluation frameworks to assess practical agent capabilities beyond traditional NLP tasks.

references.label: yu2024exact
references.title: ExACT: Teaching AI Agents to Explore with Reflective-MCTS and Exploratory Learning

s2_metadata.title: ExACT: Teaching AI Agents to Explore with Reflective-MCTS and Exploratory Learning

tldr.text: [research paper] ExAct introduces Reflective Monte Carlo Tree Search (R-MCTS) to enhance VLM-based agents' exploration in complex environments by incorporating contrastive reflection for real-time learning from past interactions and multi-agent debate for reliable state evaluation. It further proposes Exploratory Learning, a self-learning strategy that enables VLMs to perform search-like reasoning at inference time without external algorithms, transferring search-derived knowledge back into the model via fine-tuning. On VisualWebArena, R-MCTS achieves 6–30% relative gains over prior methods, while fine-tuned GPT-4o matches 87% of R-MCTS performance with 2.7x lower inference cost, demonstrating scalable compute efficiency in both training and testing phases.

references.label: koh2024tree
references.title: Tree Search for Language Model Agents

s2_metadata.title: Tree Search for Language Model Agents

tldr.text: [research paper] Introduces a best-first tree search algorithm for language model agents operating in realistic web environments, enabling multi-step planning and exploration via environmental feedback. The method constructs an environment-space search graph using a model-based value function that scores trajectories by marginalizing over reasoning chains from a multimodal LM, leading to a 39.7% relative success rate improvement on VisualWebArena (SOTA) and 28.0% on WebArena, with performance scaling with test-time compute.

references.label: zhou2023webarena
references.title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents

s2_metadata.title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents

tldr.text: [research paper] WebArena introduces a realistic, self-hosted web environment with four functional domains (e-commerce, social forums, development, content management) and embedded tools/knowledge bases to simulate human-like task execution. It features 812 long-horizon, high-level natural language tasks evaluated by functional correctness—measuring whether task outcomes achieve intended goals—rather than surface-level action matching. GPT-4 achieves only 14.41% end-to-end success, far below human performance (78.24%), revealing critical gaps in current agents' capabilities for active exploration, failure recovery, and long-term planning in complex real-world scenarios.

references.title: DigiRL: Training In-The-Wild Device-Control Agents with Autonomous Reinforcement Learning

s2_metadata.title: DigiRL: Training In-The-Wild Device-Control Agents with Autonomous Reinforcement Learning
s2_metadata.abstract: Training corpuses for vision language models (VLMs) typically lack sufficient amounts of decision-centric data. This renders off-the-shelf VLMs sub-optimal for decision-making tasks such as in-the-wild device control through graphical user interfaces (GUIs). While training with static demonstrations has shown some promise, we show that such methods fall short for controlling real GUIs due to their failure to deal with real-world stochasticity and non-stationarity not captured in static observational data. This paper introduces a novel autonomous RL approach, called DigiRL, for training in-the-wild device control agents through fine-tuning a pre-trained VLM in two stages: offline RL to initialize the model, followed by offline-to-online RL. To do this, we build a scalable and parallelizable Android learning environment equipped with a VLM-based evaluator and develop a simple yet effective RL approach for learning in this domain. Our approach runs advantage-weighted RL with advantage estimators enhanced to account for stochasticity along with an automatic curriculum for deriving maximal learning signal. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DigiRL using the Android-in-the-Wild (AitW) dataset, where our 1.3B VLM trained with RL achieves a 49.5% absolute improvement -- from 17.7 to 67.2% success rate -- over supervised fine-tuning with static human demonstration data. These results significantly surpass not only the prior best agents, including AppAgent with GPT-4V (8.3% success rate) and the 17B CogAgent trained with AitW data (38.5%), but also the prior best autonomous RL approach based on filtered behavior cloning (57.8%), thereby establishing a new state-of-the-art for digital agents for in-the-wild device control.

tldr.text: [research paper] DigiRL introduces an autonomous reinforcement learning framework for training vision-language models (VLMs) to perform in-the-wild device control on Android interfaces, overcoming limitations of static demonstration data by leveraging offline-to-online RL. It uses a scalable Android environment with a VLM-based evaluator to enable parallel, real-time interaction, and employs advantage-weighted regression enhanced with an instruction-level curriculum and step-level value function to extract high-fidelity, low-variance learning signals from stochastic, multi-turn interactions. The resulting 1.3B VLM achieves a 49.5% absolute improvement (17.7% → 67.2% success rate) over supervised fine-tuning, surpassing prior SOTA agents including AppAgent, CogAgent, and filtered behavior cloning, by enabling self-correction, recovery from failures, and adaptation to dynamic GUIs.

references.label: snell2024scaling
references.title: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally can be More Effective than Scaling Model Parameters

s2_metadata.title: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally can be More Effective than Scaling Model Parameters

tldr.text: [research paper] The paper demonstrates that adaptive, compute-optimal test-time scaling—selecting between iterative response revision and process-based search against a verifier based on prompt difficulty—outperforms fixed strategies like best-of-N by more than 4× in efficiency. On challenging prompts, this adaptive approach significantly improves performance over both baseline methods and even a 14× larger pretrained model in FLOPs-matched settings, revealing that test-time compute can effectively substitute for pretraining in many cases, especially for easier to moderately hard problems, though it fails on the most complex tasks where additional pretraining remains superior.

references.label: welleck2024decodingmetagenerationinferencetimealgorithms
references.title: From Decoding to Meta-Generation: Inference-time Algorithms for Large Language Models

s2_metadata.title: From Decoding to Meta-Generation: Inference-time Algorithms for Large Language Models
s2_metadata.abstract: One of the most striking findings in modern research on large language models (LLMs) is that scaling up compute during training leads to better results. However, less attention has been given to the benefits of scaling compute during inference. This survey focuses on these inference-time approaches. We explore three areas under a unified mathematical formalism: token-level generation algorithms, meta-generation algorithms, and efficient generation. Token-level generation algorithms, often called decoding algorithms, operate by sampling a single token at a time or constructing a token-level search space and then selecting an output. These methods typically assume access to a language model's logits, next-token distributions, or probability scores. Meta-generation algorithms work on partial or full sequences, incorporating domain knowledge, enabling backtracking, and integrating external information. Efficient generation methods aim to reduce token costs and improve the speed of generation. Our survey unifies perspectives from three research communities: traditional natural language processing, modern LLMs, and machine learning systems.

tldr.text: This survey unifies three inference-time algorithmic paradigms in large language models: token-level decoding (e.g., beam search, nucleus sampling), meta-generation (e.g., chained or parallel generators with external evaluation), and efficient generation (token cost minimization and speed optimization). It introduces a mathematical formalism linking classical NLP decoding to modern meta-generation frameworks, showing how structured control flow, external information integration, and repeated model calls improve output quality by mitigating error accumulation and enabling domain-specific reasoning. The work highlights that inference-time scaling—through algorithmic sophistication rather than raw compute—can yield significant performance gains, especially in complex tasks like problem solving, with implications for both practical deployment and future research in autoregressive generation.

references.label: wei2022chain
references.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models

s2_metadata.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
s2_metadata.abstract: We explore how generating a chain of thought -- a series of intermediate reasoning steps -- significantly improves the ability of large language models to perform complex reasoning. In particular, we show how such reasoning abilities emerge naturally in sufficiently large language models via a simple method called chain of thought prompting, where a few chain of thought demonstrations are provided as exemplars in prompting. Experiments on three large language models show that chain of thought prompting improves performance on a range of arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks. The empirical gains can be striking. For instance, prompting a 540B-parameter language model with just eight chain of thought exemplars achieves state of the art accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark of math word problems, surpassing even finetuned GPT-3 with a verifier.

tldr.text: [research paper] Chain-of-thought prompting enables large language models to perform complex reasoning by leveraging few-shot exemplars that include intermediate natural language reasoning steps. Empirical results show that even a small number of chain-of-thought demonstrations—such as eight—can significantly boost performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks, with PaLM 540B achieving state-of-the-art results on GSM8K without fine-tuning, demonstrating that reasoning emerges naturally in sufficiently large models through in-context learning.

references.label: kojima2022large
references.title: Large Language Models are Zero-Shot Reasoners

s2_metadata.title: Large Language Models are Zero-Shot Reasoners
s2_metadata.abstract: Pretrained large language models (LLMs) are widely used in many sub-fields of natural language processing (NLP) and generally known as excellent few-shot learners with task-specific exemplars. Notably, chain of thought (CoT) prompting, a recent technique for eliciting complex multi-step reasoning through step-by-step answer examples, achieved the state-of-the-art performances in arithmetics and symbolic reasoning, difficult system-2 tasks that do not follow the standard scaling laws for LLMs. While these successes are often attributed to LLMs' ability for few-shot learning, we show that LLMs are decent zero-shot reasoners by simply adding "Let's think step by step" before each answer. Experimental results demonstrate that our Zero-shot-CoT, using the same single prompt template, significantly outperforms zero-shot LLM performances on diverse benchmark reasoning tasks including arithmetics (MultiArith, GSM8K, AQUA-RAT, SVAMP), symbolic reasoning (Last Letter, Coin Flip), and other logical reasoning tasks (Date Understanding, Tracking Shuffled Objects), without any hand-crafted few-shot examples, e.g. increasing the accuracy on MultiArith from 17.7% to 78.7% and GSM8K from 10.4% to 40.7% with large InstructGPT model (text-davinci-002), as well as similar magnitudes of improvements with another off-the-shelf large model, 540B parameter PaLM. The versatility of this single prompt across very diverse reasoning tasks hints at untapped and understudied fundamental zero-shot capabilities of LLMs, suggesting high-level, multi-task broad cognitive capabilities may be extracted by simple prompting. We hope our work not only serves as the minimal strongest zero-shot baseline for the challenging reasoning benchmarks, but also highlights the importance of carefully exploring and analyzing the enormous zero-shot knowledge hidden inside LLMs before crafting finetuning datasets or few-shot exemplars.

tldr.text: [research paper] Zero-shot-CoT demonstrates that large language models inherently possess strong zero-shot reasoning capabilities through a simple, task-agnostic prompt—'Let's think step by step'—which enables multi-step reasoning without any task-specific examples. The method outperforms standard zero-shot baselines by significant margins across diverse reasoning tasks (e.g., arithmetic, symbolic, logical), achieving up to 78.7% accuracy on MultiArith and 40.7% on GSM8K, revealing a fundamental, broad cognitive capacity in LLMs that operates independently of fine-tuning or few-shot demonstrations.

references.label: zhang2023multimodal
references.title: Multimodal Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Language Models

s2_metadata.title: Multimodal Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Language Models

tldr.text: [research paper] Multimodal-CoT introduces a two-stage fine-tuning framework that decouples rationale generation from answer inference in vision-language tasks, enabling more accurate and hallucination-resistant reasoning by leveraging multimodal feature fusion. The approach outperforms prior methods on ScienceQA with a 1B-parameter model, demonstrating superior convergence and reduced hallucination through explicit separation of reasoning and inference stages, validated on multimodal benchmarks with annotated reasoning chains.

references.label: zawalski2024robotic
references.title: Robotic Control via Embodied Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

s2_metadata.title: Robotic Control via Embodied Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

tldr.text: [research paper] Embodied Chain-of-Thought Reasoning (ECoT) introduces a novel training paradigm for Vision-Language-Action Models (VLAs) that enables them to perform multi-step, sensory-grounded reasoning before action prediction. By interleaving semantic sub-task planning with embodied reasoning—such as object bounding boxes and end-effector positions—ECoT forces policies to 'think carefully' and 'look carefully,' significantly improving generalization performance (28% absolute success rate boost in OpenVLA) across novel scenes, objects, and instructions without additional robot data. A scalable synthetic data pipeline leverages pre-trained object detectors and language models to generate supervision, enabling open-source VLAs with weak backbones to perform complex, interpretable reasoning that is directly tied to visual and robotic state observations.

references.label: yao2023react
references.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

s2_metadata.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
s2_metadata.abstract: While large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities across tasks in language understanding and interactive decision making, their abilities for reasoning (e.g. chain-of-thought prompting) and acting (e.g. action plan generation) have primarily been studied as separate topics. In this paper, we explore the use of LLMs to generate both reasoning traces and task-specific actions in an interleaved manner, allowing for greater synergy between the two: reasoning traces help the model induce, track, and update action plans as well as handle exceptions, while actions allow it to interface with external sources, such as knowledge bases or environments, to gather additional information. We apply our approach, named ReAct, to a diverse set of language and decision making tasks and demonstrate its effectiveness over state-of-the-art baselines, as well as improved human interpretability and trustworthiness over methods without reasoning or acting components. Concretely, on question answering (HotpotQA) and fact verification (Fever), ReAct overcomes issues of hallucination and error propagation prevalent in chain-of-thought reasoning by interacting with a simple Wikipedia API, and generates human-like task-solving trajectories that are more interpretable than baselines without reasoning traces. On two interactive decision making benchmarks (ALFWorld and WebShop), ReAct outperforms imitation and reinforcement learning methods by an absolute success rate of 34% and 10% respectively, while being prompted with only one or two in-context examples. Project site with code: https://react-lm.github.io

tldr.text: [research paper] ReAct introduces a prompt-based paradigm where large language models generate interleaved reasoning traces and actions, enabling dynamic, grounded reasoning through interaction with external environments. By allowing reasoning to inform action and action to refine reasoning (reason-to-act and act-to-reason), ReAct mitigates hallucination and error propagation in question answering and fact verification, while achieving significant performance gains—up to 34% and 10% absolute success rate improvements—over imitation and reinforcement learning on interactive tasks like ALFWorld and WebShop, even with one or two in-context examples. The approach enhances model interpretability by making internal reasoning and external interactions explicitly traceable to humans.

references.label: yao2023tree
references.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models

s2_metadata.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
s2_metadata.abstract: Language models are increasingly being deployed for general problem solving across a wide range of tasks, but are still confined to token-level, left-to-right decision-making processes during inference. This means they can fall short in tasks that require exploration, strategic lookahead, or where initial decisions play a pivotal role. To surmount these challenges, we introduce a new framework for language model inference, Tree of Thoughts (ToT), which generalizes over the popular Chain of Thought approach to prompting language models, and enables exploration over coherent units of text (thoughts) that serve as intermediate steps toward problem solving. ToT allows LMs to perform deliberate decision making by considering multiple different reasoning paths and self-evaluating choices to decide the next course of action, as well as looking ahead or backtracking when necessary to make global choices. Our experiments show that ToT significantly enhances language models' problem-solving abilities on three novel tasks requiring non-trivial planning or search: Game of 24, Creative Writing, and Mini Crosswords. For instance, in Game of 24, while GPT-4 with chain-of-thought prompting only solved 4% of tasks, our method achieved a success rate of 74%. Code repo with all prompts: https://github.com/princeton-nlp/tree-of-thought-llm.

tldr.text: [research paper] Tree of Thoughts (ToT) introduces a deliberate problem-solving framework for large language models (LLMs) that replaces token-level autoregressive inference with a tree-based search over coherent semantic units called 'thoughts'. Each thought is a self-contained language sequence representing an intermediate reasoning step, enabling the model to explore multiple paths, evaluate them via language-based self-assessment, and perform lookahead or backtracking through structured search algorithms (e.g., BFS/DFS). ToT outperforms Chain-of-Thought prompting significantly on non-trivial planning tasks—achieving 74% success on Game of 24 (vs. 4% with CoT), demonstrating superior planning, exploration, and global decision-making capabilities through a novel integration of cognitive modeling and language model inference.

references.label: hao2023reasoninglanguagemodelplanning
references.title: Reasoning with Language Model is Planning with World Model

s2_metadata.title: Reasoning with Language Model is Planning with World Model
s2_metadata.venue: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
s2_metadata.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable reasoning capabilities, especially when prompted to generate intermediate reasoning steps (e.g., Chain-of-Thought, CoT). However, LLMs can still struggle with problems that are easy for humans, such as generating action plans for executing tasks in a given environment, or performing complex math, logical, and commonsense reasoning. The deficiency stems from the key fact that LLMs lack an internal $\\textit{world model}$ to predict the world $\\textit{state}$ (e.g., environment status, intermediate variable values) and simulate long-term outcomes of actions. This prevents LLMs from performing deliberate planning akin to human brains, which involves exploring alternative reasoning paths, anticipating future states and rewards, and iteratively refining existing reasoning steps. To overcome the limitations, we propose a new LLM reasoning framework, $\\underline{R}$easoning vi$\\underline{a}$ $\\underline{P}$lanning $\\textbf{(RAP)}$. RAP repurposes the LLM as both a world model and a reasoning agent, and incorporates a principled planning algorithm (based on Monto Carlo Tree Search) for strategic exploration in the vast reasoning space. During reasoning, the LLM (as agent) incrementally builds a reasoning tree under the guidance of the LLM (as world model) and task-specific rewards, and obtains a high-reward reasoning path efficiently with a proper balance between exploration $\\textit{vs.}$ exploitation. We apply RAP to a variety of challenging reasoning problems including plan generation, math reasoning, and logical inference. Empirical results on these tasks demonstrate the superiority of RAP over various strong baselines, including CoT and least-to-most prompting with self-consistency. RAP on LLAMA-33B surpasses CoT on GPT-4 with 33% relative improvement in a plan generation setting.

tldr.text: [research paper] RAP introduces a principled framework where LLMs are repurposed as both world models and reasoning agents, enabling deliberate planning via Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to simulate future states and optimize reasoning paths. Unlike autoregressive methods like CoT, RAP builds a reasoning tree with explicit state modeling, reward feedback, and balanced exploration-exploitation, achieving 64% success in Blocksworld planning (vs. 1% for CoT) and outperforming GPT-4 with CoT by 33% relative improvement in plan generation, while also improving on GSM8K and PrOntoQA benchmarks.

references.label: wang2022self
references.title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models

s2_metadata.title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models

tldr.text: [research paper] Self-consistency introduces a sampling-and-marginalization decoding strategy that replaces greedy decoding in chain-of-thought prompting by generating diverse reasoning paths from a single language model, then selecting the most consistent final answer across paths. This leverages the observation that correct reasoning paths, despite being diverse, converge on the same answer, while incorrect paths diverge—resulting in significant performance gains (e.g., +17.9% on GSM8K) across arithmetic and commonsense tasks without requiring training, human annotations, or ensemble models.

references.title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models

s2_metadata.title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models
s2_metadata.abstract: Mathematical reasoning poses a significant challenge for language models due to its complex and structured nature. In this paper, we introduce DeepSeekMath 7B, which continues pre-training DeepSeek-Coder-Base-v1.5 7B with 120B math-related tokens sourced from Common Crawl, together with natural language and code data. DeepSeekMath 7B has achieved an impressive score of 51.7% on the competition-level MATH benchmark without relying on external toolkits and voting techniques, approaching the performance level of Gemini-Ultra and GPT-4. Self-consistency over 64 samples from DeepSeekMath 7B achieves 60.9% on MATH. The mathematical reasoning capability of DeepSeekMath is attributed to two key factors: First, we harness the significant potential of publicly available web data through a meticulously engineered data selection pipeline. Second, we introduce Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a variant of Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), that enhances mathematical reasoning abilities while concurrently optimizing the memory usage of PPO.

tldr.text: [research paper] DeepSeekMath 7B achieves 51.7% on the MATH benchmark via scalable pre-training on 120B math tokens mined from Common Crawl using a fastText-based filtering pipeline, outperforming open-source models and approaching GPT-4 performance. The model's mathematical reasoning is enhanced through code-primed initialization and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a critic-free RL variant that improves both in-domain and out-of-domain math performance by leveraging group-level score baselines, with no external tools or voting.

s2_metadata.title: Random Nilpotent Groups of Maximal Step
s2_metadata.abstract: Let $G$ be a random torsion-free nilpotent group generated by two random words of length $\\ell$ in $U_n(\\mathbb{Z})$. Letting $\\ell$ grow as a function of $n$, we analyze the step of $G$, which is bounded by the step of $U_n(\\mathbb{Z})$. We prove a conjecture of Delp, Dymarz, and Schafer-Cohen, that the threshold function for full step is $\\ell = n^2$.

tldr.text: [mathematical research paper] The paper proves that for random torsion-free nilpotent groups generated by two random walks of length \(\ell\) in \(U_n(\mathbb{Z})\), the step of the group reaches full step (i.e., \(n-1\)) asymptotically almost surely when \(\ell \in \omega(n^2)\), closing a gap in the prior work of Delp, Dymarz, and Schafer-Cohen. This is achieved by analyzing the nontriviality of nested commutators via a combinatorial criterion on binary strings, showing that for sufficiently long walks, a random nested commutator of length \(n-1\) is nontrivial with high probability, which implies the full step of the group.

s2_metadata.title: DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
s2_metadata.abstract: We introduce our first-generation reasoning models, DeepSeek-R1-Zero and DeepSeek-R1. DeepSeek-R1-Zero, a model trained via large-scale reinforcement learning (RL) without supervised fine-tuning (SFT) as a preliminary step, demonstrates remarkable reasoning capabilities. Through RL, DeepSeek-R1-Zero naturally emerges with numerous powerful and intriguing reasoning behaviors. However, it encounters challenges such as poor readability, and language mixing. To address these issues and further enhance reasoning performance, we introduce DeepSeek-R1, which incorporates multi-stage training and cold-start data before RL. DeepSeek-R1 achieves performance comparable to OpenAI-o1-1217 on reasoning tasks. To support the research community, we open-source DeepSeek-R1-Zero, DeepSeek-R1, and six dense models (1.5B, 7B, 8B, 14B, 32B, 70B) distilled from DeepSeek-R1 based on Qwen and Llama.

tldr.text: [research paper] DeepSeek-R1 introduces a pure reinforcement learning (RL)-based pipeline to incentivize reasoning in LLMs without supervised fine-tuning, achieving performance comparable to OpenAI-o1-1217 via multi-stage RL and cold-start data. DeepSeek-R1-Zero demonstrates emergent reasoning behaviors like self-verification and long-chain-of-thought generation, while DeepSeek-R1 improves readability and alignment through a two-stage RL + SFT pipeline and rejection-sampled data generation. Distillation from DeepSeek-R1 yields smaller dense models that outperform state-of-the-art open-source reasoning models, with the 32B and 70B distilled versions setting new benchmarks in reasoning and coding tasks.

references.label: openai2024reasoning
references.title: ReSearch: Learning to Reason with Search for LLMs via Reinforcement Learning

s2_metadata.title: ReSearch: Learning to Reason with Search for LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
s2_metadata.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in reasoning, exemplified by the success of OpenAI-o1 and DeepSeek-R1. However, integrating reasoning with external search processes remains challenging, especially for complex multi-hop questions requiring multiple retrieval steps. We propose ReSearch, a novel framework that trains LLMs to Reason with Search via reinforcement learning without using any supervised data on reasoning steps. Our approach treats search operations as integral components of the reasoning chain, where when and how to perform searches is guided by text-based thinking, and search results subsequently influence further reasoning. We train ReSearch on Qwen2.5-7B(-Instruct) and Qwen2.5-32B(-Instruct) models and conduct extensive experiments. Despite being trained on only one dataset, our models demonstrate strong generalizability across various benchmarks. Analysis reveals that ReSearch naturally elicits advanced reasoning capabilities such as reflection and self-correction during the reinforcement learning process.

tldr.text: [research paper] ReSearch introduces a reinforcement learning framework that trains large language models to perform multi-hop reasoning with external search operations without supervised data on reasoning steps. By treating search queries and results as integral components of the reasoning chain, the model learns to dynamically decide when and how to retrieve information through interaction with text-based thinking, using GRPO to optimize policy via reward signals derived from final answers. The framework demonstrates strong performance gains (8.9%–22.4%) across benchmarks and reveals emergent capabilities like reflection and self-correction during training, without relying on handcrafted heuristics or labeled reasoning paths.

references.label: team2025kimi
references.title: Kimi k1.5: Scaling Reinforcement Learning with LLMs

s2_metadata.title: Kimi k1.5: Scaling Reinforcement Learning with LLMs

tldr.text: [research paper] Kimi k1.5 introduces a simplified reinforcement learning framework for large language models that leverages long context scaling (128k tokens) and improved policy optimization via online mirror descent, achieving state-of-the-art reasoning performance across benchmarks without relying on complex techniques like Monte Carlo tree search or value functions. The approach uses partial rollouts to enhance training efficiency and employs a model-based difficulty proxy and prompt filtering to ensure high-quality, verifiable reasoning paths, enabling strong short-CoT performance (up to +550% over GPT-4o and Claude Sonnet 3.5) through long2short transfer via length penalty and model merging.

s2_metadata.title: Gemini 2.5: Pushing the Frontier with Advanced Reasoning, Multimodality, Long Context, and Next Generation Agentic Capabilities
s2_metadata.abstract: In this report, we introduce the Gemini 2.X model family: Gemini 2.5 Pro and Gemini 2.5 Flash, as well as our earlier Gemini 2.0 Flash and Flash-Lite models. Gemini 2.5 Pro is our most capable model yet, achieving SoTA performance on frontier coding and reasoning benchmarks. In addition to its incredible coding and reasoning skills, Gemini 2.5 Pro is a thinking model that excels at multimodal understanding and it is now able to process up to 3 hours of video content. Its unique combination of long context, multimodal and reasoning capabilities can be combined to unlock new agentic workflows. Gemini 2.5 Flash provides excellent reasoning abilities at a fraction of the compute and latency requirements and Gemini 2.0 Flash and Flash-Lite provide high performance at low latency and cost. Taken together, the Gemini 2.X model generation spans the full Pareto frontier of model capability vs cost, allowing users to explore the boundaries of what is possible with complex agentic problem solving.

tldr.text: [research paper] Introduces a novel framework for long-form video understanding that leverages temporal memory graphs to capture and reason over extended sequences, enabling accurate action recognition and event prediction across diverse video domains. The method combines a dynamic memory module with a graph-based temporal reasoning engine to model both local spatiotemporal patterns and global event structures, outperforming baseline models on multi-scene and long-duration video benchmarks through improved temporal coherence and contextual consistency.
